text: Abstention rule. When the evidence quote is empty or the cited utterance cannot be reconstructed, the model should mark the trait as insufficiently supported instead of inferring HIGH or LOW from the label prior.

metadata.name: abstention_and_insufficient_evidence
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Abstention rule. A single weak cue such as a short agreement, greeting, thanks, or apology is not enough to infer a stable Big Five trait unless it is paired with clearer behavior or repeated across contexts.

metadata.name: abstention_and_insufficient_evidence
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Abstention rule. Polite social formulas can support an Agreeableness state only weakly. Do not infer high Agreeableness from routine politeness unless the text shows concern, respect, helping behavior, forgiveness, or trust beyond etiquette.

metadata.name: abstention_and_insufficient_evidence
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Abstention rule. A situational anxious reaction can indicate a high Neuroticism state, but it should not be treated as stable high Neuroticism without repeated worry, rumination, emotional volatility, or stress vulnerability.

metadata.name: abstention_and_insufficient_evidence
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Abstention rule. One dialogue can justify a state-level judgment, but trait-level judgment requires a pattern across states or dialogues. If the available dialogue is narrow and context-specific, lower confidence or abstain.

metadata.name: abstention_and_insufficient_evidence
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Aggregation rule. A personality state is a short-term pattern of thought, feeling, or behavior in a concrete situation. It should be used as evidence for a trait, not confused with the trait itself.

metadata.name: state_trait_aggregation_rules
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Aggregation rule. A trait reflects a distribution of states over time. Repeated states across different contexts provide stronger trait evidence than an isolated event.

metadata.name: state_trait_aggregation_rules
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Aggregation rule. State-trait disagreement is valid: a person can show a temporary state that points opposite to their stable trait. Treat disagreement as uncertainty to aggregate, not as annotation noise.

metadata.name: state_trait_aggregation_rules
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Aggregation rule. When inferring traits, prioritize repeated evidence, cross-context consistency, and facet coverage over vivid but isolated utterances.

metadata.name: state_trait_aggregation_rules
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory

text: Aggregation rule. When retrieved states point to conflicting trait polarities, reduce confidence and explain the conflict instead of forcing a single high-confidence conclusion.

metadata.name: state_trait_aggregation_rules
metadata.citation: Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015; Whole Trait Theory
